name: Life Science: Classification, Evolution, Genetics, Physiology, Nutrition, Health & Hygiene, Human Diseases

researchNotes: One genuinely real, directly-relevant hit (the genetic-disorder mismatch question) plus 2 stashed real hits from earlier research. The 27-question web-sourced set covered far more ground than one topic needs — picked ~15 of the clearest, most well-established facts (skipping COVID-19-specific items since those are time-sensitive 2020-2022-era 'current affairs' content likely stale for a 2026+ exam, and skipping one item — a 'Kaya Kalp award' fact — that I couldn't independently verify with confidence). Later extended with a user-provided TNPSC revision set (originally Q12-Q40) covering vitamin deficiencies, hormones, blood/heart physiology, kidney, and common diseases; a few duplicate facts within that source set (e.g. rickets mentioned twice, insulin/pancreas mentioned twice, iron/anaemia mentioned twice) were consolidated into single reading items rather than repeated.

reading.intro: This is the broadest clause in Unit I, spanning classification, genetics, physiology, nutrition, and disease. Real exam evidence favors mismatch-style and match-the-following questions over plain definitions, so several items below are framed that way.

qa.question: Real exam: Which of these genetic-disorder pairings is a MISMATCH? Down's Syndrome–Autosomal trisomy of 21st chromosome; Haemophilia–Sex Linked; Klinefelter Syndrome–XO Complement; Turner Syndrome–Female with retarded growth.
qa.answer: Klinefelter Syndrome–XO Complement is the mismatch. Klinefelter Syndrome is actually XXY (an extra X chromosome in males); XO (a missing X chromosome) is Turner Syndrome, which correctly presents as female with retarded growth — that pairing in the list is actually correct, the error is specifically in mislabeling Klinefelter as XO.

qa.question: Real exam: What are minisatellites, in DNA terms?
qa.answer: Short repeating DNA sequences consisting of 10-60 base pairs (bp) long base pairs — used in DNA profiling/fingerprinting.

qa.question: Real exam: Which statements about antibiotics are true — (i) active against a large number of microorganisms, (ii) enhance fungal growth, (iii) minimum inhibitory concentration is effective against different microorganisms?
qa.answer: (i) and (iii) are true. (ii) is false — antibiotics target bacteria, they don't 'enhance' fungal growth (and many antibiotics are themselves derived FROM fungi, e.g. Penicillin).

qa.question: What is Streptomycin active against, and where does Penicillin come from?
qa.answer: Streptomycin is active against Gram-negative bacteria. Penicillin is an antibiotic obtained from a blue-green mold (Penicillium, which is itself classified as a Fungus).

qa.question: What causes liver cirrhosis, and what category of drug is nicotine?
qa.answer: Cirrhosis of the liver is caused by chronic (long-term) alcohol intake. Nicotine belongs to the Stimulants group of drugs.

qa.question: What bacterium is most associated with stomach ulcers, and what disease does Bacillus anthracis cause?
qa.answer: Helicobacter pylori causes most ulcers. Bacillus anthracis causes Anthrax in cattle and humans.

qa.question: How does Typhoid spread, and what is Dengue's transmission cycle?
qa.answer: Typhoid spreads through contamination of drinking water and milk. Dengue follows a Man→Mosquito→Man transmission cycle.

qa.question: Which plasma protein is used in antibody formation?
qa.answer: Globulin (not albumin, fibrinogen, or thrombin — those have other blood-plasma roles).

qa.question: What first-aid combination should be given to an electrocuted person with cardiac arrest?
qa.answer: All of the above: external cardiac compression massage, mouth-to-mouth resuscitation, AND cardio-pulmonary resuscitation (CPR) together — not just one of these alone.

qa.question: Name a fresh-water habitat example and the driest habitat type, per basic classification.
qa.answer: Fresh water habitat: a pond. Driest habitat: deserts.

qa.question: What is the main function of stomata in plants, and what are the primary organs of photosynthesis?
qa.answer: Stomata's main function is transpiration (water vapour release/gas exchange). The primary organs of photosynthesis are leaves.

qa.question: Which vitamin deficiency causes night blindness, and which causes scurvy?
qa.answer: Vitamin A deficiency causes night blindness. Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy (bleeding gums, poor wound healing) — Vitamin C is abundant in citrus fruits.

qa.question: Which vitamin deficiency causes rickets/osteomalacia, and which causes beriberi?
qa.answer: Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets in children (soft, weak bones) and osteomalacia in adults. Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) deficiency causes beriberi (weak muscles, nerve problems).

qa.question: Which vitamin deficiency impairs blood clotting?
qa.answer: Vitamin K deficiency — Vitamin K is essential for blood clotting (coagulation).

qa.question: What disease results from insulin deficiency, and which gland produces insulin?
qa.answer: Insulin deficiency causes Diabetes Mellitus. Insulin is produced by the Pancreas, which regulates blood sugar levels.

qa.question: Which gland is called the 'Master Gland', and why?
qa.answer: The Pituitary gland is called the Master Gland because it controls the functioning of other endocrine glands in the body.

qa.question: Which blood group is the universal donor and which is the universal recipient? Why does blood group O carry that property?
qa.answer: O Negative (O⁻) is the universal donor and AB Positive (AB⁺) is the universal recipient. Blood group O carries no A or B antigen on its red blood cells, so it can be given to anyone without triggering an immune reaction.

qa.question: What are the respective functions of RBCs, WBCs, and platelets in blood?
qa.answer: Red Blood Cells (RBCs/Erythrocytes) transport oxygen via haemoglobin. White Blood Cells (WBCs/Leucocytes) fight infection and provide immunity. Platelets (Thrombocytes) are responsible for blood clotting.

qa.question: Which blood vessel is the exception that carries oxygenated blood, and which heart chamber has the thickest wall?
qa.answer: The pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to the heart — an exception, since veins normally carry deoxygenated blood (correspondingly, the pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood, the opposite of the usual arterial rule). The Left ventricle has the thickest wall, since it pumps blood to the entire body.

qa.question: Which mineral deficiencies cause Goitre and Anaemia respectively?
qa.answer: Iodine deficiency causes Goitre (affects the thyroid gland). Iron deficiency causes Anaemia — iron is essential for haemoglobin formation.

qa.question: Which microorganism converts milk into curd?
qa.answer: Lactobacillus, a beneficial bacterium, ferments milk into curd.

qa.question: Which mosquitoes transmit Malaria, Dengue, and Filariasis respectively?
qa.answer: The female Anopheles mosquito transmits Malaria. The Aedes mosquito transmits Dengue. The Culex mosquito transmits Filariasis.

qa.question: Where is bile produced and stored, and where does protein digestion begin?
qa.answer: Bile is produced by the Liver and stored in the Gall bladder. Protein digestion begins in the Stomach, via the enzyme Pepsin.

qa.question: Which enzyme in saliva digests starch?
qa.answer: Salivary amylase (Ptyalin) digests starch, beginning carbohydrate digestion in the mouth.

qa.question: Which organ filters blood to form urine, and what is its basic functional unit?
qa.answer: The Kidney filters blood and forms urine; its basic functional unit is the Nephron.

qa.question: Which hormone is called the 'Emergency Hormone', and where is it produced?
qa.answer: Adrenaline (Epinephrine) is called the Emergency Hormone. It is produced by the Adrenal gland and prepares the body for 'fight or flight'.

qa.question: Which gland secretes Thyroxine, and what element does it contain?
qa.answer: The Thyroid gland secretes Thyroxine, which contains Iodine.

qa.question: Which organ is affected by Hepatitis, and what are its main types?
qa.answer: Hepatitis affects the Liver. Its main types are Hepatitis A, B, C, D, and E.

qa.question: What disease does HIV cause, how does it spread, and how does it NOT spread?
qa.answer: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) causes AIDS. It spreads through unprotected sexual contact, infected blood transfusion, sharing contaminated needles, and mother-to-child transmission during pregnancy, birth, or breastfeeding. It is NOT spread by handshakes, hugging, sharing food, or mosquito bites.

questions.prompt: Which of the following genetic-disorder pairings is a MISMATCH?
options: Down's Syndrome – Autosomal trisomy of 21st chromosome
Turner Syndrome – Female with retarded growth
questions.explanation: Klinefelter Syndrome is XXY, not XO — XO is Turner Syndrome.

questions.prompt: Minisatellites are DNA sequences consisting of base pairs in which approximate range?
questions.explanation: 10-60 base pairs long repeating sequences.

questions.prompt: Which statement about antibiotics is FALSE?
options: They are active against a large number of microorganisms
Minimum inhibitory concentration is effective against different microorganisms
They enhance the growth of fungi
All of the above are true
questions.explanation: Antibiotics do not enhance fungal growth — many are even derived from fungi.

questions.prompt: Streptomycin is mainly active against which type of bacteria?
questions.explanation: Gram-negative bacteria.

questions.prompt: Chronic intake of which substance most commonly causes cirrhosis of the liver?
questions.explanation: Alcohol.

questions.prompt: Penicillin, a widely used antibiotic, is obtained from:
questions.explanation: Penicillium, a fungus (blue-green mold).

questions.prompt: Ulcers are mostly caused by infection from which bacterium?
questions.explanation: Helicobacter pylori.

questions.prompt: Typhoid is most commonly spread through:
questions.explanation: Contaminated water/milk.

questions.prompt: Dengue virus follows which transmission cycle?
questions.explanation: Man-Mosquito-Man.

questions.prompt: Which plasma protein plays the key role in antibody formation?
questions.explanation: Globulin.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the main function of stomata in a plant leaf?
questions.explanation: Transpiration (water vapour/gas exchange).

questions.prompt: A pond is a classic example of which type of habitat?
questions.explanation: Fresh water habitat.

questions.prompt: Deficiency of which vitamin causes night blindness?
questions.explanation: Vitamin A deficiency causes night blindness.

questions.prompt: Deficiency of which vitamin causes scurvy?
questions.explanation: Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy — bleeding gums and poor wound healing.

questions.prompt: Deficiency of which vitamin causes rickets in children?
questions.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults.

questions.prompt: Deficiency of which vitamin causes beriberi?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) deficiency causes beriberi — weak muscles and nerve problems.

questions.prompt: Which vitamin is essential for blood clotting?
questions.explanation: Vitamin K is essential for blood clotting (coagulation).

questions.prompt: Deficiency of insulin leads to which disease?
questions.explanation: Insulin deficiency causes Diabetes Mellitus; insulin is produced by the Pancreas.

questions.prompt: Which gland is known as the 'Master Gland'?
questions.explanation: The Pituitary gland controls other endocrine glands, earning it the name 'Master Gland'.

questions.prompt: Which blood group is known as the universal donor?
questions.explanation: O Negative (O⁻) has no A/B antigen, making it the universal donor.

questions.prompt: Which blood group is known as the universal recipient?
questions.explanation: AB Positive (AB⁺) can receive blood from all groups, making it the universal recipient.

questions.prompt: Which blood component is primarily responsible for clotting?
questions.explanation: Platelets (Thrombocytes) are responsible for blood clotting.

questions.prompt: Which blood cells are primarily responsible for fighting infection?
questions.explanation: White Blood Cells (Leucocytes) provide immunity and fight infection.

questions.prompt: Which blood cells transport oxygen throughout the body?
questions.explanation: RBCs contain haemoglobin, which transports oxygen.

questions.prompt: Which blood vessel is an exception in carrying oxygenated blood?
questions.explanation: The pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to the heart, unlike other veins which carry deoxygenated blood.

questions.prompt: Which chamber of the human heart has the thickest wall?
questions.explanation: The left ventricle has the thickest wall since it pumps blood to the entire body.

questions.prompt: Deficiency of iodine causes which disease?
questions.explanation: Iodine deficiency causes Goitre, affecting the thyroid gland.

questions.prompt: Deficiency of iron causes which disease?
questions.explanation: Iron is essential for haemoglobin formation; its deficiency causes Anaemia.

questions.prompt: Which microorganism converts milk into curd?
questions.explanation: Lactobacillus ferments milk into curd.

questions.prompt: The bite of an infected female Anopheles mosquito causes which disease?
questions.explanation: Anopheles transmits Malaria; Aedes transmits Dengue; Culex transmits Filariasis.

questions.prompt: Which organ produces bile, and where is it stored?
options: Liver; stored in the gall bladder
questions.explanation: Bile is produced by the Liver and stored in the Gall bladder.

questions.prompt: Which enzyme in saliva digests starch?
questions.explanation: Salivary amylase (Ptyalin) begins starch digestion in the mouth.

questions.prompt: What is the basic functional and filtration unit of the kidney?
questions.explanation: The Nephron is the kidney's basic functional unit, filtering blood to form urine.

questions.prompt: Which hormone is known as the 'Emergency Hormone'?
questions.explanation: Adrenaline (Epinephrine), from the adrenal gland, prepares the body for 'fight or flight'.

questions.prompt: Thyroxine, which contains iodine, is secreted by which gland?
questions.explanation: The Thyroid gland secretes Thyroxine, which contains iodine.

questions.prompt: Hepatitis primarily affects which organ?
questions.explanation: Hepatitis (types A-E) affects the Liver.

questions.prompt: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) causes which disease?
questions.explanation: HIV causes AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome), spread via unprotected sex, infected blood/needles, and mother-to-child transmission — not by casual contact.
